We are most likely to succeed in attempts to glorify God and be useful to people when we learn from experience that we cannot do anything ourselves; when our complete dependence is placed on him, and our only expectation comes from him. Moses had been expecting what God would do; now he will see what he will do. God wanted to be known by his name Jehovah, which means a God performing what he promised and finishing his own work. God intended their happiness: I will take you to me as my people, a special people, and I will be your God. We need not ask for more, we cannot have more, to make us happy. He intended his own glory: You shall know that I am the Lord. These good and comforting words should have revived the discouraged Israelites and made them forget their misery; but they were so consumed by their troubles that they did not pay attention to God’s promises. By indulging discontent and worry, we deprive ourselves of the comfort we might have, both from God's word and from his care, and remain without comfort.

Moses’ faith was so weak that he could scarcely be kept to his work. Ready obedience always comes from the strength of our faith. Though our weaknesses ought to humble us, they ought not to discourage us from doing our best in any service we have to do for God. When Moses repeats his unsuccessful arguments, he is no longer argued with; instead, God gives a charge to both the children of Israel and to Pharaoh. God’s authority is enough to answer all objections and binds everyone to obey without complaining or arguing.

Moses and Aaron were Israelites; raised up from among their brothers, as Christ also would be, who was to be the Prophet and Priest, the Redeemer and Lawgiver of the people of Israel. Moses returns to his story and repeats the charge God had given him to deliver his message to Pharaoh, and he expresses his objection to it. Those who have spoken without thinking ought to reflect on it with regret, as Moses seems to do here. “Uncircumcised” is used in Scripture to note the unsuitableness there may be in anything to answer its proper purpose; just as the carnal heart and depraved nature of fallen people are wholly unsuitable for serving God and for the purposes of his glory. It is profitable to place no confidence in ourselves; all our sufficiency must be in the Lord. We can never trust ourselves too little, or our God too much. I cannot do anything by myself, said the apostle, but I can do all things through Christ who gives me strength.
